impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court held that because the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), the patent laws did not shield Ingevity from antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of patent holders who engage in tying arrangements can use antitrust counterclaims to challenge exclusionary licensing practices that extend patent power into unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle patent licenses with requirements to purchase unpatented products face significant antitrust liability if the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses, even where the patent itself is later invalidated.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents are abstract at Alice step one. The court also ruled that damages based on Norton's foreign sales were improperly awarded and reversed the contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel. The case was remanded for further proceedings on Alice step two and reconsideration of enhanced damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against patent assertions gain a stronger basis to challenge cybersecurity and software patents as abstract under § 101, and the foreign-sales damages ruling limits patent damages exposure for products sold outside the U.S.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the software and cybersecurity space face heightened risk that their patents will be invalidated as abstract, even after surviving pre-trial challenges and obtaining substantial jury verdicts.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held on cross-appeal that GoTV's streaming media patents are ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment including a $2.5 million jury award on the '715 patent. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content specifications to device capabilities. The ruling required entry of judgment for Netflix across the entire case.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering server-side content adaptation, reinforcing that generic claims about tailoring content to device capabilities are patent-ineligible.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting patents on content delivery and device-tailored streaming methods face increased risk of § 101 invalidation, even after securing favorable jury verdicts at trial.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on enforcement of a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment for both parties, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct during patent prosecution (failure to disclose prior CYMAX documents) and Global Tubing's Walker Process fraud/attempted monopolization claim. The case was remanded for trial on both issues, keeping alive significant antitrust and patent unenforceability theories.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors can pursue Walker Process antitrust claims when patent holders allegedly obtain patents through fraud, potentially recovering antitrust damages. The ruling lowers the barrier to reaching trial on such claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face the risk that failures to disclose material prior art during prosecution could support both unenforceability due to inequitable conduct and antitrust liability for attempted monopolization.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing a recombinant nucleic acid molecule encoding a specific AAV capsid protein with a heterologous non-AAV sequence are patent-ineligible as directed to a natural phenomenon under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that claims to engineered host cells containing specific recombinant DNA constructs are not directed to a natural phenomenon. The court found the claimed cultured host cells are man-made compositions distinct from anything occurring in nature, preserving patent protection for gene therapy platform technologies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotech and gene therapy companies gain stronger footing for patent protection of engineered biological materials, with the court recognizing that man-made host cells with recombinant constructs are not natural phenomena under § 101.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to use AAV-based gene therapy vectors may face renewed infringement claims now that these foundational patents survive eligibility challenges.

impacts.caseName: Genuine Enabling Technology LLC v. Sony Group Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's failure to map all elements of a complex means-plus-function corresponding structure to an accused product warrants summary judgment of noninfringement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of noninfringement for Sony on GET's patent covering computer input devices. The court held that because GET and its expert failed to account for many elements within the logic block identified as the corresponding structure for the 'encoding means' limitation, the infringement case was fatally deficient. This reinforces the rigorous requirements for proving infringement of means-plus-function claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against means-plus-function patent claims benefit from the court's strict requirement that patent holders must map every element of the identified corresponding structure to the accused product.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting means-plus-function claims face heightened evidentiary burdens and risk summary judgment if their infringement analysis fails to address all elements of the corresponding structure disclosed in the specification.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and common law right of access.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket confidentiality designation for all questionnaire responses in trade investigations is incompatible with the statute (19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a, 1677f) and does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The Commission must now evaluate confidentiality designations on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies involved in trade disputes and their competitors may gain greater access to information submitted in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, improving transparency in international trade litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit proprietary business information in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of public disclosure, potentially exposing competitively sensitive data that was previously automatically protected.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) when the complainant requested a general exclusion order, and whether the appeal of the no-violation finding as to active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely and affirmed the ITC's issuance of only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The court held the ITC did not abuse its discretion under § 1337(g)(1) in entering an LEO rather than a general exclusion order, even where the complainant sought broader relief for trademark infringement of its 3D Marks covering Classic Clog shoes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners can still obtain limited exclusion orders against defaulting respondents in ITC trademark investigations based on statutory presumptions, without needing to present full infringement contentions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad general exclusion orders at the ITC face significant hurdles when active respondents prevail on the merits, as limited exclusion orders against only defaulting parties may provide insufficient commercial protection.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute (15 U.S.C. § 9009c) creates a mandatory obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act, entitling eligible applicants to damages when the SBA failed to award grants in statutory order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that the RRF statute's use of 'shall' in directing the SBA to award grants in the order applications were received created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act. Over 300 restaurants and businesses who applied on the first day but were bypassed in favor of later applicants may proceed with their claims for unpaid grant amounts.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses that were denied government grants or benefits due to agency failure to follow statutory disbursement requirements have a viable path to recover damages in the Court of Federal Claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability for billions in damages when agencies fail to follow mandatory statutory grant-making procedures, establishing precedent for similar claims across other government benefit programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions to the Board on exercising discretionary IPR non-institution authority are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions, and the instructions do not purport to bind the Director.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to issue and modify guidance on IPR institution discretion without formal rulemaking, allowing rapid policy adjustments in patent review proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners cannot challenge the PTO's discretionary denial policies through APA procedural challenges, limiting their ability to ensure access to inter partes review as a patent-challenge mechanism.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft on the basis that Exafer had no remedy. The court found the district court improperly excluded the expert report, which quantified damages for infringement of patents related to virtual network optimization technology used in Microsoft's Azure platform. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders gain protection against overly aggressive Daubert exclusions of damages experts, ensuring they can present their damages case to a jury even when methodological disputes exist.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits face the prospect of trials on damages theories that might otherwise have been excluded, increasing litigation costs and settlement pressure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement of spinal surgery tool patents and erred in granting JMOL for the defendant.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed JMOL for DePuy and remanded for a new trial, holding the district court abused its discretion in excluding both of the patent holder's expert witnesses on induced infringement of spinal derotation tool patents. The ruling enables the patent holder to present his full infringement case, including testimony on how surgeons use DePuy's devices in configurations that allegedly infringe.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting method-of-use claims against medical device manufacturers gain reassurance that relevant expert testimony will not be improperly excluded, preserving their ability to prove induced infringement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face increased exposure to induced infringement claims where expert testimony on surgeon use of their products can now be presented at retrial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the asserted claims of a crop harvester height-control patent are invalid as indefinite due to the means-plus-function 'control means' limitation.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of the '395 patent are invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification adequately disclosed the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation. The court remanded for further proceedings on the remaining issues, reviving patent infringement claims against Deere's header sensor kits for agricultural equipment.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from the court's recognition that describing components as 'conventional in design and operation' can be sufficient to disclose corresponding structure, reducing the risk of indefiniteness invalidation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment manufacturers and other technology companies face revived patent infringement claims that had been dismissed on indefiniteness grounds.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the jury's finding of nonobviousness and the damages award for infringement of a pre-lit artificial tree patent with coaxial trunk connectors were supported by substantial evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL motion on obviousness and its motion for a new trial on damages, upholding the jury's findings that Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with integrated electrical connectors was not obvious and that damages were properly calculated. The case involved Polygroup's 'Quick Set' trees accused of infringing Willis's patented coaxial trunk connector design.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product industries receive confirmation that jury verdicts on nonobviousness and damages will be upheld where supported by substantial evidence, even for relatively simple mechanical inventions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product companies face sustained infringement liability and damages for products that incorporate patented connector technologies, even where prior art exists in adjacent fields.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of multimedia data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of the patent holder's complaint for failure to state a claim, finding that the asserted web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims, which covered systems allowing participants to view recorded portions of a conference while a live session continued, were held to be directed to abstract ideas without an inventive concept at Alice step two.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies offering video conferencing and streaming platforms gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering basic time-shifting and asynchronous viewing functionality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting software patents related to web conferencing and media streaming face increasingly difficult § 101 challenges, even at the pleading stage before discovery.
